Everywhere in the Quran, everywhere in the Quran, there is an essence of his statement in the prophet sallall.aii Wasam said that when Allah concluded the creation of the heavens and the earth and rose above his throne, he wrote in a book which he has with him to this day, "My mercy has exceeded my anger. My mercy has superseded my anger." And you'll find this pattern everywhere in the Quran. Anytime the mercy of Allah and the punishment of Allah are mentioned somewhere, there's an indicator in there somewhere. Sometimes it's very subtle, very nuanced, but there is always an indicator in there of you will find that his insistence on mercy whenever it's mentioned in the Quran alongside his anger and punishment, you'll find nuanced in there something subtle, sometimes very subtle that carry carries the spirit of my mercy has superseded my anger. summary: This lecture discusses the purposes of the Quran as revealed by Allah, focusing on two main objectives: warning humanity of severe punishment for those who reject the Quran, and providing glad tidings and hope to believers who perform righteous deeds with the promise of paradise. The speaker emphasizes the eternal nature of paradise and contrasts it with the temporal pleasures of this world, which are inevitably ended by death. The lecture highlights a recurring theme throughout the Quran where Allah's mercy is portrayed as superseding His anger, a concept supported by a hadith in which the Prophet Muhammad stated that Allah wrote, 'My mercy has exceeded my anger.' The speaker points out that this merciful theme appears subtly throughout Quranic verses whenever punishment is mentioned alongside reward.
suggested_title: The Purposes of the Quran: Warning and Glad Tidings
catchy_title: When Allah's Mercy Exceeds His Anger
